questions.source: Email sample topics; mock system design
questions.question: Designing a URL shortener at architectural level (not coding), what is the primary reason to use a distributed unique ID or hash for short codes instead of sequential integers?
options: Avoid predictable/guessable URLs and enable independent ID generation across servers without a single coordination point
Sequential integers are always faster than hashes for database indexing
Hashes eliminate the need for any persistent storage layer
Short codes must be exactly 128 characters for security
questions.explanation: URL shortener interviews test architectural ID strategy: base-62 encoding of distributed IDs (Snowflake, UUID hash) avoids a global counter bottleneck and reduces enumeration attacks. Sequential IDs are simpler but expose predictability and coordination costs at scale.

questions.question: A URL shortener receives 100:1 read-to-write ratio. Which v1 read-path architecture is most appropriate to discuss?
options: Cache hot mappings in memory/CDN; async write path to durable store with redirect on cache miss
Write-through to database on every redirect with no caching to guarantee freshness
Store all mappings only in application memory on one server
Use a graph database because URLs form a linked structure
questions.explanation: Read-heavy asymmetry drives cache-first redirect paths — a canonical pattern from AOSA. Writes are rare enough to tolerate slightly slower persistence; reads need sub-10ms redirects. Architectural choice centers on cache placement and invalidation, not linked-list algorithms.

questions.question: You need a distributed rate limiter enforcing 1000 requests/minute per API key across hundreds of stateless servers. Which architectural approach scales best?
options: Centralized counter store (Redis/Memcached) with atomic increment and TTL windows, or token bucket per key
Local in-memory counters on each server with no coordination
A relational table with row-level lock on every request
Rate limiting only at the client SDK with no server enforcement
questions.explanation: Distributed rate limiting requires shared state or approximate coordination. Centralized counters with sliding/fixed windows are the standard architectural pattern. Per-server counters over-allow by N×; DB row locks don't scale to request-rate granularity.

questions.question: Where should rate limiting sit architecturally for a public API with multiple microservices behind a gateway?
options: At the API gateway/edge before routing to backends, with optional finer-grained limits per service
Only inside each microservice with no edge enforcement
Only in the database layer after queries execute
Rate limiting is unnecessary if you use HTTPS
questions.explanation: Edge/gateway placement protects the entire fleet from abuse early, reduces wasted downstream work, and centralizes policy. Per-service limits add nuance but edge enforcement is the architectural first line — consistent with AOSA's reverse proxy and load balancer patterns.

questions.question: Designing a metrics/logging pipeline for 500K events/sec, which v1 ingestion architecture should you propose?
options: Agents on hosts batch and ship to a partitioned message bus; consumers write to time-series or columnar storage
Synchronous RPC from each host directly to a single SQL database row per event
Store raw logs only on local disk with no aggregation path
Email logs to operators in real time for manual review
questions.explanation: High-volume metrics pipelines use async ingestion (Kafka/Pub-Sub), batching, and partitioned consumers — the MapReduce/streaming pattern from email topic lists. Sync per-event DB writes cannot sustain hundreds of thousands of events/sec architecturally.

questions.question: Dashboard queries aggregate metrics over 30-day windows across millions of time series. Which storage/query architectural trade-off fits?
options: Pre-aggregate at ingest into rollups (1m, 1h, 1d); query layer selects granularity by time range
Store only raw events and scan all rows on every dashboard load
Keep metrics in application heap memory for fastest reads
Use a graph traversal engine because metrics form dependencies
questions.explanation: Metrics systems architecturally separate hot raw retention from rolled-up long-term storage. Pre-aggregation at ingest trades write complexity for bounded query cost — standard in observability pipeline design, not LeetCode-style data structure tricks.

questions.source: AOSA distsys caching; email topic list
questions.question: A distributed cache sits in front of a database. Which cache invalidation architecture handles updated records correctly under concurrent writers?
options: Write-through or cache-aside with explicit invalidation/version keys; TTL as safety net
Infinite TTL with no invalidation because caches are always faster
Delete the entire cache cluster on every single write globally
Cache only static configuration; never cache dynamic user data
questions.explanation: Cache-aside with explicit invalidation or write-through on mutation is the canonical pattern from AOSA. TTL alone risks stale reads; global flush doesn't scale. Architectural discussion focuses on consistency window acceptance, not implementing LRU in code.

questions.question: A distributed cache must scale beyond one machine's memory. Which sharding approach should you describe architecturally?
options: Consistent hashing over cache keys across nodes with virtual nodes for even distribution
Randomly assign each request to any cache node regardless of key
Replicate the full dataset to every node always
Shard by request arrival time instead of key
questions.explanation: Consistent hashing (DHT concept from email topic list) enables horizontal cache scale with minimal remapping on node add/remove. Architectural interviews test why key-based partitioning beats random or full replication for large working sets.

questions.question: Architecturally, what is the core challenge of a polite large-scale web crawler?
options: Maintaining a scalable URL frontier with deduplication, per-host rate limits, and prioritized fetch scheduling
Parsing HTML with regular expressions faster than browsers
Storing crawled pages only in process memory on one machine
Fetching every URL on the internet simultaneously without coordination
questions.explanation: Web crawler system design centers on frontier queue architecture, bloom-filter dedup, politeness per domain, and distributed fetch workers — not HTML parsing code. Email lists crawlers among canonical large-system topics.

questions.question: A crawler stores billions of fetched pages. Separating metadata from content architecturally means:
options: Index URL, checksum, fetch time, and size in a queryable store; store raw page bytes in object/blob storage
Store everything in a single JSON column in one SQL table
Keep all pages in the crawler's heap and discard after indexing
Use only a filesystem with no index because paths are unique
questions.explanation: Metadata/blob separation (from AOSA image-hosting and email rubric) lets you scale cheap object storage for bytes and fast indexes for lookup. This pattern recurs across crawlers, photo apps, and notification attachments.

questions.source: Email sample topics; AOSA message queues
questions.question: A notification system must deliver email, SMS, and push from one API call. Which v1 architecture decouples channels appropriately?
options: API enqueues notification events; channel-specific workers consume from queues with retry and dead-letter handling
API synchronously calls every third-party provider before returning to the user
Single monolithic worker sends all channels sequentially in one thread on one machine
Users poll an endpoint every second to check for notifications
questions.explanation: Notification systems are canonical async fan-out designs: durable queue, per-channel workers, idempotent delivery. Sync third-party calls on the API path violate latency and reliability goals — standard message-queue pattern from AOSA.

questions.question: Users configure per-channel notification preferences and quiet hours. Where should preference evaluation architecturally occur?
options: Before enqueue or at a routing service that filters/suppresses before channel workers execute
Only in the mobile client after all messages are already sent
Hard-coded in each channel worker with no shared preference store
Preferences are irrelevant for v1 system design
questions.explanation: Preference routing is an architectural gate between intent and delivery — typically a dedicated service or pipeline stage reading a preference store. Late filtering wastes provider quota and annoys users; client-only filtering is unreliable.

questions.question: Designing REST APIs for a large user-generated content service, which principle best supports evolvability?
options: Resource-oriented URLs, explicit versioning, pagination cursors, and consistent error schemas
RPC-style endpoints named after internal function names with no versioning
Return HTTP 200 with error details in body for all failure cases
Embed all related entities deeply nested in every response always
questions.explanation: Email rubric allows API/payload design at pseudocode level. REST best practices for large systems emphasize stable resource models, version headers/paths, cursor pagination at scale, and machine-readable errors — architectural interface design, not implementation.

questions.question: A payment API may receive duplicate POST requests due to client retries. Which architectural API design mitigates double charges?
options: Require client-supplied idempotency keys; server deduplicates within a TTL window
Use GET instead of POST for all mutating operations
Disable all client retries globally
Rely on the database primary key alone without application-level dedup
questions.explanation: Idempotency keys are standard architectural API design for unreliable networks — conceptual exactly-once from email topic list. This is an interface contract decision, not a coding exercise about HTTP libraries.

questions.source: AOSA image hosting; Systems Architecture video SA-V3
questions.question: An image hosting service stores photos up to 20 MB each with searchable tags and upload timestamps. Why separate metadata from blob storage architecturally?
options: Metadata needs indexed queries; blobs need cheap durable object storage with CDN delivery — different access patterns and cost profiles
Blobs must live in SQL BLOB columns for transactional guarantees on every byte
Metadata and blobs must always co-locate on the same disk for performance
Separation is only needed below 1 GB total storage
questions.explanation: SA-V3 mentions separating data vs metadata. AOSA's image hosting example is the canonical reference: SQL/NoSQL for searchable metadata, object store + CDN for bytes. Interviewers test whether you match storage tier to access pattern.

questions.question: A startup's messaging app evolves: v1 single DB, v2 read replicas, v3 sharded multi-region. What architectural driver most likely triggered v2?
options: Read traffic exceeded single-primary capacity; replicas offload reads while writes still hit primary
Developers wanted to use a new programming language
Users requested dark mode in the UI
Legal requirement to delete all message history
questions.explanation: v1→v3 evolution under load is a standard interview narrative: vertical scale → read replicas → shard/partition → multi-region. Each step addresses a measured bottleneck. NALSD's iterative design expects you to tie version changes to concrete scaling or reliability triggers.

questions.source: Email topic list — indexes; AOSA distsys
questions.question: Autocomplete search over product names must return suggestions within 50 ms for 10M products. Which architectural index choice fits (not a coding question)?
options: Prefix index or trie-like structure (or search engine) optimized for prefix queries, possibly sharded by prefix
Full table scan on every keystroke sorted alphabetically
Store products in a linked list for sequential traversal
Client-side brute force over a downloaded 10M JSON file
questions.explanation: Email lists indexes as a systems architecture topic. Autocomplete is an architectural index problem: prefix indexes, dedicated search engines (Elasticsearch), or trie shards — not linked-list reversal. Choose structure based on query pattern at scale.

questions.question: A system needs O(1) lookup of user sessions by session token with no range queries. Which architectural storage choice is most appropriate to discuss?
options: Distributed hash/key-value store keyed by session token
B-tree index on a relational table when only point lookups are needed
Graph database because sessions relate users to actions
Append-only log scan for every authentication request
questions.explanation: Email explicitly says test *when* hash vs index vs graph is the right architectural choice — not LeetCode implementations. Pure point lookups by opaque key map to hash/KV designs; B-trees add value for range scans you don't need.

questions.question: v2 of your API introduces breaking payload changes while v1 clients still exist. Which evolution strategy preserves operability?
options: Run v1 and v2 endpoints concurrently with deprecation timeline; gateway routes by version header or path
Force all clients to upgrade simultaneously with no backward compatibility
Silently change v1 response schema and fix client bugs in production
Abandon API versioning and embed schema version in every field name
questions.explanation: Large-system evolution requires parallel API versions and explicit deprecation — part of v1→v3 thinking under real-world constraints. NALSD iteration applies to interfaces as well as infrastructure; breaking all clients at once is not viable at scale.

questions.question: The interviewer asks you to design 'a system like Twitter's home timeline.' Which first architectural fork best separates concerns?
options: Clarify read vs write ratio and fan-out strategy (push fan-out on write vs pull on read vs hybrid)
Immediately choose a specific database brand without requirements
Design the mobile UI color scheme for tweet cards
Implement a red-black tree for storing tweets in memory
questions.explanation: Canonical social feed design hinges on the fan-out architectural choice — push, pull, or hybrid — driven by follower distribution and read/write ratio. This is the high-level fork interviewers expect before storage or API details; it tests architectural judgment, not coding.
